content.assessment: Status quo is the politically 'safe' default because the original 1989-90 settlement was a cross-ideological coalition outcome (church, conservative, liberal), meaning no single actor currently owns reform failure. However, this same finding shows the settlement's legitimacy rested on active multi-bloc buy-in, not inertia; passive monitoring without action risks converting quiet acceptance into visible distrust, especially as ministerial acknowledgment of burdens without a corrective response has already been flagged as eroding legitimacy.
opportunities: Buys time to build a broader coalition before attempting more disruptive reform, potentially avoiding premature politicization
strengths: Requires no new coalition-building since it preserves the outcome of an already-settled cross-ideological 1989-90 bargain
Avoids triggering the demonstrated high symbolic-salience opposition mobilization capacity seen even against modest reform proposals
No structural change means no new veto-constituency mobilization from church schools or divided unions
threats: Continued or worsening segregation could eventually force reactive, less carefully designed reform under crisis conditions rather than deliberate policy design
weaknesses: Monitoring-without-correction has a documented history of not altering trajectory, undermining the proposal's own stated equity-tracking rationale
Ministerial rhetoric acknowledging problems while defending inaction is identified as a specific driver of eroding public legitimacy
Persistent large socioeconomic performance gaps under status quo create an accumulating legitimacy liability even absent formal opposition
